name: Botswana

colors.symbolism: water — specifically rain, vital in this semi-arid country; reflected in the national motto Pula ('let there be rain')
the majority black African population; together with white, the racial harmony of the people
the white minority and other peoples; together with black, racial harmony and the stripes of the zebra (the national animal)

description: A light-blue field crossed horizontally by a wide black stripe in the centre, fimbriated above and below by narrow white stripes. The black-and-white centre evokes the markings of a zebra.
history: Adopted on 30 September 1966, the day Botswana gained independence from the United Kingdom (formerly the Bechuanaland Protectorate). The colours and design were chosen specifically to contrast with the red-white-blue of neighbouring apartheid-era South Africa: blue and white instead of red, with the black-and-white centre symbolising racial harmony in deliberate opposition to apartheid.

features.symbolism: the central black stripe with white fimbriation on a light-blue field represents the harmony and racial cooperation between the people of Botswana, echoing the stripes of the zebra
